pattern: Any subgraph satisfying both paths must have some meeting node where the two forward paths merge into a single path to dest, so trying every candidate meeting node and summing the two forward distances plus the shared tail distance finds the optimum.
hints: The two required paths don't have to stay separate all the way to dest - they can converge at some intermediate node and share the rest of the route.
For a candidate meeting node m, the cheapest subgraph through it costs dist(src1 to m) + dist(src2 to m) + dist(m to dest); try every node as the candidate meeting point and take the minimum.

Run Dijkstra once forward from src1, once forward from src2, and once on the reverse-edge graph from dest (so its distances read as node-to-dest); then it's one pass over all nodes summing the three arrays.

edge_inputs.description: two sources converge at an intermediate node before reaching dest
no path exists from one source to dest at all
minimum n, a direct triangle of edges
cheapest individual paths to dest do not share a meeting point, so paths must be tried through every candidate node, not just the direct one
an edge exists but only in the wrong direction, so it must not be usable for the forward paths
parallel edges between the same pair of nodes with different weights, the cheaper one must win
no edges at all, so no subgraph can exist unless src1 or src2 already equals dest (they never do per constraints, so answer is -1)

stress.description: random sparse directed graph of up to 2000 nodes, src1/src2/dest chosen distinct and edges weighted so paths usually exist
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(3, min(size, 2000))
    edges = []
    m = min(n * 3, 100000)
    seen_pairs = set()
    for _ in range(m):
        u = rng.randrange(n)
        v = rng.randrange(n)
        if u == v:
            continue
        if (u, v) in seen_pairs:
            continue
        seen_pairs.add((u, v))
        w = rng.randrange(1, 100001)
        edges.append([u, v, w])
    nodes = list(range(n))
    rng.shuffle(nodes)
    src1, src2, dest = nodes[0], nodes[1], nodes[2]
    return [n, edges, src1, src2, dest]
stress.note: edges deduplicated by direction so the cheapest-parallel-edge trap never silently double counts; distinct src1/src2/dest as guaranteed
